summary: Gruber reviews Konfabulator, a $25 JavaScript runtime engine for Mac OS X that runs small visual 'widgets' created by Kaleidoscope co-creator Arlo Rose. While he finds the concept neat and praises JavaScript as the scripting language choice, he criticizes two major decisions: mandatory skinning with no option for native Aqua UI elements, and the absence of an IDE for creating widgets. He argues the business model is backwards — the runtime should be free to maximize the audience for widgets, while the IDE should be the paid product. Ultimately, he sees Konfabulator as prioritizing visual flash over developer productivity, targeting pixel tweakers rather than programmers.
key_points: Konfabulator uses JavaScript as its scripting language, which Gruber considers a much better choice than the originally planned Perl, especially for beginning programmers
Mandatory skinning with bitmap images is a significant design flaw — there's no way to use native Aqua UI elements, making even simple widgets require extensive pixel work
The lack of an IDE is Konfabulator's most perplexing omission, forcing developers to hand-edit XML with pixel offsets and manage projects without proper tooling
The business model is inverted: charging $25 for the runtime limits the audience for widget creators, when the runtime should be free and the IDE should be the paid product
Konfabulator is clearly targeted at the Kaleidoscope crowd — visual tweakers — rather than programmers who want to build useful small utilities
money_quotes: JavaScript is a terrific scripting language. Certainly it is most famous for client-side web browser scripting, but it's really a general purpose scripting language.
What I don't like, not one bit, is that "skinning" is not an option — it is mandatory.
It just seems silly not to make it easy to create a non-skinned default UI.
Konfabulator is clearly geared to appeal to the Kaleidoscope crowd: tweakers who prefer to spend their time hacking pixels in Photoshop than hacking their actual source code.
I wouldn't write it for Konfabulator, because I wouldn't want to limit the audience to those who've paid $25 for Konfabulator.
It makes more sense to me to give away the runtime engine, and let everyone run widgets for free. Then charge money for the IDE.
Creating Konfabulator widgets isn't hard in the way that differential equations are hard; it just looks like a pain in the ass.
I would have been happier if their work for version 1.0 had gone toward automating the construction of Konfabulator widgets, at expense of visual effects.
key_insight: Konfabulator's fatal flaw is prioritizing visual appeal over developer experience and getting its business model backwards — the runtime should be free to grow the ecosystem, while the creation tools should be the product worth paying for.
